speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's the 23rd of July, 1995. The survivor is John Ullmann. The interviewer is Libby Raichman. The city is Sydney in New South Wales, Australia. And the interview [INAUDIBLE] will be conducted in English. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Libby Raichman, L-I double B-Y, surname R-A-I-C-H-M-A-N. I'm interviewing John Ullmann, U double L-M-A double N in Sydney, Australia. Today is the 23rd of July, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, John. Can you please tell me when and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the 28th of December, 1926, in a village called Alsovalko.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-L-S-O--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --dash [SIGHS] V-A-L-K-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Romania. It has a Romanian name of Valcau de Jos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have any other names, your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just those two names.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me about your childhood in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I had a very happy childhood. I was free. We had close-by mountains, winter sports, river in, in the end of the backyard, big garden with-- full of fruit trees. And there was no choice of schools, only public school. And when I-- in 1938, my parents decided to send me to a Jewish high school. It was about 110 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's go back to your-- to you early childhood. First of all, in your town, were the winters severe? Did you have severe winters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Severe winters, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Snow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the river at the back of your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: The river was completely frozen over. You couldn't just walk through it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you learn to, to skate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skate on the river, yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --until--

speaker: interviewer
text: --good?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, we had to clean the snow. And some [? did ?] without skates, just-- they had to-- like horseshoes. And, and the back of the heel, it just went through the ice. It was like a mirror.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the ice didn't crack?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the ice was about 8 or-- 8 or 10 inches deep, frozen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had good fun on the river in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the neighbor's children all came down, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the children. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. And the snow on the mountains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the snow lasted usually-- it fall in the beginning of December, or during December. And it lasted till end of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- did you ski, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had proper skis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Proper skis, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Were there other-- were you the-- were there other children in your family, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you, you did all these sports with the neighboring-- neighbors' children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Actually, I, I did ski. I took always a friend home from school--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who came in the winter holidays, which we had only two or three weeks, Christmastime. And always, someone, someone from my class accompanied me. So the two of us, we went skiing. Because nobody had skis, only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call that, sledge?

speaker: interviewer
text: Little sledge.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sledges everybody had, but not ski. I had the skis from-- with me in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: You used to hire them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had your own. So the children were--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- in high school-- when I went to high school, we-- I was a member of a club which owned a hut in the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we could use that free of charge as-- about 10 days a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did those sports become your sports? Were those your-- those winter sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I skied even after the war, but not now.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, I think you can't forget it. If I would go, I would succeed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you would.

speaker: interviewee
text: I might fall a few times, but that's all. I, I did try ice skating in Sydney-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which-- I, I went all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Still, you don't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like bicycle riding. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. John, tell me about your primary school years. Those years you spent in your village, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your primary school years.

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they were mostly-- a majority were Romanian children, about 60%. And about, oh, could be 35% were Hungarian children. And would be about two, two or three Jewish kids local. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amongst them. How many Jewish families did you have in your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 12 families-- 12 to 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you-- did-- and were you close? Were the 12 families close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were all quite on good terms, all of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? we were. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a little synagogue. And where did you have that? Did you have a special shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, special-- that, that was next door to our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next door to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, next door to our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We, we only had visiting rabbis from other places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they just come for Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur? Or were they there on other occasions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes on other occasions, too. When you had a, a brit or wedding, they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if somebody died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or if somebody died, they, they, they came to officiate.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where did they come from? Which was the nearest big town to your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: They come from-- there were three places. One was Kraszna. And one was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Kraszna?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-R-A-S-Z-N-A. They had a rabbi there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there, there was a very large village with more Jewish people called Nagyfalu--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell--

speaker: interviewee
text: --which was only--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --10 Kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And they had their, their own rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their-- Nagyfalu means, in English, 'big village'--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --translated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was the name of it. They had a permanent rabbi. And then they had the rabbi in Somlyo. That was [INAUDIBLE], 18 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Somlyo?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-O-M-L-Y-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And how did the rabbi get to your village? How-- what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: By horse.

speaker: interviewer
text: --transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: --horse carriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: A horse-drawn carriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Horse-drawn carriage, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Closed in, or just a wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the wagon, not closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just an open wagon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Open wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came in the snow and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Snow, we had sledge--

speaker: interviewer
text: Horse--

speaker: interviewee
text: --horse-drawn sledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So John, your early years were very happy years for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were old-- what about your Hebrew, your Jewish education in your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who taught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't recall the name. But the cheder was in our place. We, we had--

speaker: interviewer
text: In your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not our house. We had a house in, in our backyard, which was transformed from-- it was originally agricultural machinery in part of it, and even livestock in the other end. And then two rooms were kept, which was the closest to our house. The end of that, that building was kept for cheder. And we had our classes practically every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, more or less, how many children came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about six or eight children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the cheder teacher, was he from the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The, the cheder teacher, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] actually, he, he came from somewhere else. But then he resided here permanently for years.

speaker: interviewer
text: In, in your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in the village, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he taught you to read the-- read the siddur and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Tanakh, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when you got to the end of primary school-- so-- well, just a minute. You were going to public school in the morning and cheder in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the afternoon, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got to high-school age, what did you do? Did you stay on in your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: In high school?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents stayed all the time there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the high school 110, 120 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a place-- Oradea. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-R-- O-R-D-E-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oradea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a city of about 60,000 people. And it had two Jewish schools. One was an Orthodox religious school, which had only four classes. And one was a Reformed school, where I went, which had eight classes. So they didn't want to put me in the religious one because I had to come over anyway to this one in the fifth one.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the, the religious Jewish school only went up to a certain grade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up to certain grade. And if they wanted to continue, they had to come over to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, they couldn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: --have been too happy about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't too happy about it because we were not religious in that school.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, were your parents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They kept the Shabbat and a kosher home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had-- well, we-- he inherited some land. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Close to your house or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were close. Some were not close. And he had, say-- my grandfather had spirit factory. And he was also--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean a liquor factory, that kind of spirits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they call it spirit. Plums and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --like a normal spirit, 96 degree-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: --but not methylated. Right. I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: A proper spirit. And he also had cattle for, for feeding-- for slaughter, but he-- they were using from the mills. They, they had flour mill, oil, oil mill, and the spirits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents had a flour mill and an oil mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was part of the other family.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they, they were feeding these with grain, the, the animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the mill.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the-- then that was in-- after Second World War, in, in 1928, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: After the First World War.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the First World War--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they, they-- these lands were taken away. And they were given some coupons, which wasn't worth anything. And what was a lot people-- it was about-- we were left about 40 acres, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were left with 40 acres.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the Second World War, you still owned that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father owned part of it because he kept selling it all the time. And then in the last three years, actually, he, he-- we had very little left, no more machinery. And he had a, a shop-- he opened a shop in the same building, which sold groceries and hardware.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your father sell the land, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I really-- I really don't know what's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult for him to keep it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, those 40 were not enough. You could produce enough for yourself, but not much to sell. But the grandfather had much more. And that was taken away. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And your father in his shop sold food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No food.

speaker: interviewer
text: No food.

speaker: interviewee
text: No food, except essentials of grocery, like salt, sugar--

speaker: interviewer
text: And flours.

speaker: interviewee
text: --flours, that sort of thing. But it mainly were tools. And I can remember they did bartering.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, some of the people came from distant villages, hours to buy sugar or salt. And they didn't have money. They bartered eggs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fresh eggs or other products they produced, and they wanted sugar instead. So you just had to calculate how many-- what was the price of the egg, and how much sugar they can buy for so many eggs. [LAUGHS] That went on for years like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's interesting. Did your father, besides his work, did he have time for any hobbies or other interests-- did he have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's hobby was-- he, he did woodworking, his hobby. And I'm doing it, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really? That's interesting. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the last three years during the Second World War, he actually leased the building, which had the cheder and the livestock and the [INAUDIBLE] machinery. He leased it to a Turkish company, who employed Ukrainian-Russian people from Bessarabia. And they produced dried prunes from fresh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what you call that prunes-- Angelina.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the Angelina plum.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Angelina plums, the blue ones, which you eat. And they even made delicacies, I remember. They-- after a day, it, it was put into, into this huge stove, which were in the ground, 2-meter-diameter pipes running in-- and above were a structure of timber, with trays in it and doors. Each door had about five trays, and were five, five-- 3 times 5 vertically and all along, could have been about 25 feet each.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a lot of plums went in there. And those plums-- you, you could-- after a day, 24 hours, you could take the plums out, and squeeze the seed out through one end of it, and replace it with, with a walnut-- Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with a walnut seed and keep drying it further. I shouldn't have told you. This is trade secrets. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And this was all going on at the back of your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they, they kept it till, till the-- we were deported from that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was seasonal because they only used it for about three months in a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were the plums grown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the whole area.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your--

speaker: interviewee
text: People came with the plums in horse-drawn carriages. And they were selling them to this company.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They went, went onto the scale with the livestock, with the carriage, and then when empty. And they just deducted the difference-- [LAUGHTER] --and paid for, for whatever was the weight of a plum.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very interesting. Did you-- can you tell me a bit about your mother, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother was a very nice lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian name was Lenke. And she-- her Hebrew name was Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Steinberger. And she come from an area another 100 kilometers away from a different direction, north from our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what town she came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Halmeu in the Romanian language, or Halmi in Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it in Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-A-L-M-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a little township close to Szatmar, which was a Jewish center.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very religious Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --center, wasn't it? All right. And her family was also religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can you tell me, did your mother have any other interests besides housekeeping and looking after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She, she read a lot. And books had to be brought from 18 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the bigger villages.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, from the township, from Somlyo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a library there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you, you could get five-- four or five books at a time from--

speaker: interviewer
text: And she got them on a regular basis.

speaker: interviewee
text: --public library. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did your mother do? Did she have any other interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Besides reading?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a household.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: She helped in the shop. And she was painting.

speaker: interviewer
text: She used to paint.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very interesting. Do you have anything of your mother's that she painted from those early days? Do you have anything of hers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one painting I have, which-- a still life.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: An oil painting she painted in 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did she-- when did she paint, at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, day-- daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She painted on natural light only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we, we didn't have electricity in this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No running water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the water from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From a well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she carried it herself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could carry it, for drinking water.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and no hot water, of course, in the house, either.

speaker: interviewee
text: No hot water. There was just a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bath, which was heated by steam from the flour mill, which was driven by steam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the, the flour mill actually-- the mill itself was driven by water. But next to it was another mill for sunflowers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for oil.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was driven by steam. And they used the skin of the sunflower to fire the steam engine. Part of the-- of the skin of, of, of the shell--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --went into a separate chamber. And they just used shovels to put it on, on the fire, mixed with timber. [LAUGHS] And the, the-- that bath was made-- you went down steps, deep, level with that small river, which was driven the-- which was driving the flour mills. So you had fresh water from that river. This was-- diverted part of the river only, enough to, to drive two big--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the river, of the-- of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Beretyo, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-R-E-T-Y-O. And it was a good, good river, full of fish. And people even drank from the river who didn't have a well or they had--

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the-- in the-- in the three months or the two months when the-- when the snow fell and the river became iced over, does that mean that the mills didn't work at that time because there was no water to drive the mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The, the diverted part was fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that, that couldn't freeze from the speed of, of it. But the main river was frozen over, which it was slow in parts. In certain places where it was fast, it couldn't freeze even that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where it was rapids. But you could see ice coming down from the big timber, which were-- was catching the water and driving the mill. It, it was a wheel, maybe four meter in diameter and about one meter wide. And the river-- the-- when the frost came and part of it was frozen like candles sticking on the side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it looked like a stalagmite in a-- in a cave. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. John--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --do you remember who you played with when you were a child? Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I, I got one, one-- two friends still alive. And they're living in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you played with as a child at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they also-- they were caught up in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were with me in concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they both--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated together, same carriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's interesting. John, when you left to go to high school, was that difficult for you to, to leave home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I want-- I wanted to change. The life in a, in bigger city, I like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and what about your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never had brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're an only child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live when you went away to high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Oradea, in my aunt's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was she related to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And you stayed with her for the years--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with her. She never had children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years were you with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years, and after the war again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, after the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you came back to stay with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, can you tell me, how old were you when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in-- that was-- 15 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 15.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I was born in '26, and the war broke out in 1940. The war with Russia broke out '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So with France, that was a quick-- a quick work over, [LAUGHS] practically.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to-- I'm interested in something. When the war broke out-- when the war started in 1939, it didn't come to Hungary immediately. Did you feel the effects of the war in Poland and what was going on in Germany? Did it affect you at all in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we met groups of-- not in my village, but in Oradea, where, where I went to high school. I could remember on Fridays, they used to have transports coming from Vienna. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- one of the large bakeries used to bake extra bread and bring them out to the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Jewish people who went to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those people who came on those transports were traveling on trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Traveling on trains going towards the Black Sea Port.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were going to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to speak to any of them? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, yeah. Some of them spoke Hungarian. They were Austrian Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Austrian Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were traveling across Europe to, to the Black Sea and on to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Black Sea. And then they, they had passports. And, and some had, of course, entry permits, British entry permits to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember at what stage that was? Do you remember what, more or less, what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it must have been '42-- '41, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What, what else do you remember? What happened in your school, for example, when the Germans first started spreading themselves into Europe? Do you remember any effect on your schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in a Jewish school. And other schools had a very-- in the same town, we had about six high schools, mixed. And some were co-educational, and some weren't. Some were Romanian language. Some were-- had Hungarian language. And many Jewish people had two schools. And they went to those two schools as--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, they had two schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: One, one was an Orthodox high school, and one was Reformist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they had a choice of two schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a choice of two schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so besides your schools-- the, the two Jewish schools, there were four other schools in the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four, four boys' schools. And then they had separate girls' schools, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were very many schools. Some were state schools. Some were-- belonged to different churches.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so there were quite a number of schools in the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did anything change for you with the German occupation of, of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not--

speaker: interviewer
text: In school, for example.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much of Europe. Only when the Germans entered Hungary, things changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But until then, did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Until then--

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you have any-- feel any of the effects of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was effects in the war, because we had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anti-Semitism was growing, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- high schools, you couldn't feel it so much. But universities was limited. Jewish people could only attend, I think, 6% of the total. That was already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before the war, even. The Hungarians introduced that.

speaker: interviewer
text: A quota  of Jews for universities.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember changed? Did you, did you, did you sense that your teachers in your-- in a Jewish school, you obviously had Jewish teachers. Did you sense that the teachers were afraid or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- we didn't, we didn't sense-- not all the teachers were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had non-Jewish teachers as well. Well, we had Romanian teachers, and one Hungarian teacher for gymnastics and physical culture, and a Romanian one for music. All the rest were Jewish teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your school-- what was the language, the main language of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1938, it was Romanian. In 1940, it became Hungarian. And parts of the curriculum which we went through in 1938, we had to repeat three, four years later because it was a different curriculum under Hungarian system, especially languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you speak Hungarian and Romanian?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents at home-- what did they speak to you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents spoke Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't know any Romanian?

speaker: interviewee
text: They know, but they couldn't-- they didn't speak properly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived in Romania, and you spoke Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that quite common?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's common. You had-- you had certain villages that were pure Hungarian population in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's even now the same. They've got 2 and 1/2 million Hungarians living there today.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, what about-- you said there were some signs of anti-Semitism besides the entrance to university. Do you remember any incidents in your town-- in the town where you were studying when you were living with your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. First, we had some fights in the street, daytime. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Personally and others from the classroom, from school. And we had uniforms. And marked-- we had distinguished caps, each school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if, if one of us was attacked because he went in a street-- side street-- on his own, and he had other high school students from another school who were beating him up. Or if we were more than one, then they wouldn't touch. [LAUGHS] So they picked on a single student.

speaker: interviewee
text: They picked on, on single ones, which they were sure they could beat him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was non-Jewish children walking the street. They'd pick on a Jewish child walking alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were also high school students, mainly the same age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were quite a few-- lots of fights.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they wouldn't-- it didn't happen the other way round, that Jewish children would pick a fight with a non-Jewish child? Did you know of any of those cases? Or was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That happened, too. It happened--

speaker: interviewer
text: And was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because they-- someone beat his friend up a few days earlier. So he, he just knew who he was, and he was just talking to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would children-- did children usually go home from school in groups or pairs at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They, they also-- see, in the middle of the Second World War, our school was requisitioned by the government and made into a military hospital, with wounded Hungarian soldiers from the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put into a girls' school for afternoon shift. So they finished 1 o'clock or 2:00. And we started from 2:00 till 7:00 at night. It was quite fun because you could leave notes-- [LAUGHTER] --and meet the girl who was sitting in the same bench in the morning. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we met some girls like that, that way, too. And that went on for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that went on till, till we were deported. We, we never got our school building back--

speaker: interviewer
text: You never got your old school from--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because the war was still going. And the-- they were-- they're having more and more wounded people from the front line.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when-- was Romania-- Romania was at that time-- that part of Romania was then part of Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. In 1940, it became Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: The part of Romania where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Transylvania. It became Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's where your parents lived and where you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- northern Transylvania became part of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of, of Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of Hungary, that part of Romania. Now, do you remember when the first troops came into your town, into Oradea? When did the war actually come to Oradea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the, when the war broke out with the Soviet Union, then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it all happen for you? Do you remember the day that it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, actually, the days when German troops moved down through our township by train and, and mechanized units to-- before they attacked Greece and Yugoslavia. They built up their forces close to the-- those borders. They went through Hungary and Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they passed through your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And some of them stopped for two, three hours in, in the station. And some of these German soldiers came out and bought goods. Some even signed coupons, hand-written coupons by themselves, that Hitler would pay for it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't want to-- any, any Jewish shop who, who they entered just kept quiet and were happy to get rid of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape 1 of the interview with John Ullmann. It's the 23rd of July, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
